After my usual TOL today, I sat down to meditate on the stave Luis, drew it in the air in front of me and got a nice bright silver glowing stave. Hey. I like this. After a monkey-mind muddled meditation, I thought to collapse the stave. Presto! I got a glowing silver ball. Umm? Then a humanoid figure of the same molten metal waved from the ball and signaled, “What next?” Uh-oh. I said, “You’d better head back to the Hoard.” Wave and sci-fi beam-up.
My imagination could have cooked that scene up. Hot metal, I assure you, has a personality… so does cold, but a different… never mind… but – The scene had the real feel, although it was without the sense of altered state.

At this page is the full list of all of the requests for prayer that have recently appeared at ecosophia.net and ecosophia.dreamwidth.org, as well as in the comments of the prayer list posts (printable version here, current to 10/20). Please feel free to add any or all of the requests to your own prayers.

If I missed anybody, or if you would like to add a prayer request for yourself or anyone who has given you consent (or for whom a relevant person holds power of consent) to the list, please feel free to leave a comment below.

* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests, selected from the fuller list.


May Patrick's mother Christine's vital energy be strengthened so she can continue healing at home without need for more surgical operations.

May both Monika and the child she is pregnant with both be blessed with good health and a safe delivery.

May Mary's sister have her auto-immune conditions sent into remission, may her eyes remain healthy, and may she heal in body, mind, and spirit.

May Marko have the awareness and strength to constructively deal with the situation.

May 5 year old Max be blessed and protected during his parents' contentious divorce; may events work out in a manner most conducive to Max's healthy development over the long term.

May Corey Benton, whose throat tumor has grown around an artery and won't be treated surgically, and who is now able to be at home from the hospital, be healed of throat cancer.

May HippieVikings's baby HV, who was born safely but has had some breathing concerns, be filled with good health and strength.

Guidelines for how long prayer requests stay on the list, how to word requests, how to be added to the weekly email list, how to improve the chances of your prayer being answered, and several other common questions and issues, are to be found at the Ecosophia Prayer List FAQ.

If there are any among you who might wish to join me in a bit of astrological timing, I pray each week for the health of all those with health problems on the list on the astrological hour of the Sun on Sundays, bearing in mind the Sun's rulerships of heart, brain, and vital energies. comments.title: Oracular Encounters
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I haven't seen much discussion of seers and human oracles (as opposed to those contacted through divination) in your posts, so I wanted to ask you about my encounters with such. Significant points in my spiritual development have coincided with chance encounters with people expressing strange and unexpected insights.

Several years ago I was at a party with some major occult vibes in a magically significant location. At this event there was a guy wandering around in a chaotic mental state, clearly affected by some combination of exotic substances, speaking in non sequiturs. He didn't appear aggressive at all and was tolerated by the people there. He approached me to talk several times and I humored him. Among other things he repeatedly asked me if I embodied a specific esoteric archetype, one of the major arcana from the tarot. He must have asked me this strange question ten times. It just so happened that I had gone through a kind of grueling, years-long initiation focused on this archetype, the nature of which I came to understand at the end of the ordeal. I thought the situation was absurd on its face but at the same time I wanted to reply to his question by saying "Yes, I am."

The other incident is something that happened just the other day. I was walking in my neighborhood and I noticed something wedged into the juniper hedge in front of a house. It was a pink envelope with a wax seal on it, labeled "Collective Conscious." I wasn't sure if that was supposed to be the sender or the receiver. I thought it was probably intended for whoever lived in the house so I left it, but I had a feeling it may have also been intended for a passer-by to pick up. I made a mental note that I would check back later and see if it was still there.

A few days later I remembered the envelope. There had been a bit of rain in the meantime and I figured it probably wasn't there anymore but I walked over to check anyway. It was missing from the hedge and then I noticed that it had fallen to the ground with slight water damage. Satisfied that the people in the house weren't concerned with it, I picked it up. Inside was a note handwritten in red pen telling me that I was in the process of awakening to my potential and fulfilling my work from past lives. I picked up the letter just before some heavy rain came in that would have turned it to mush. Like the erratic guy at the party, the writer seemed to know some specific things about my path. I'm not disclosing all of what was communicated to me in these transmissions out of a strong sense that their full contents are intended only for me.

Have you or any readers had these kind of oracular encounters? How would you interpret these experiences, and are there any books or other sources you would recommend to give some context? I take these encounters as strong signs that I'm on the right path, but I also look twice at anything that appears certain. The guy at the party seems like a classic example of someone in an ecstatic trance state. I also wonder about the letter writer -- could the letter be considered a form of magical initiation with no contact between the giver and receiver? It was placed along a sidewalk that lots of people traverse, so the fact it wasn't picked up over several days gives me a strong sense I was meant to get it. I also wonder if it could be intended to have a harmful or parasitic effect by encouraging egotism or the like.

Thanks for reading.

replies.title: Re: Oracular Encounters
replies.content: Hmm! No, those aren't experiences I've had. If I did have them, I'd do divination to try to get some sense of how seriously to take them. Maybe some of the others here have additional ideas!

comments.title: Días de los Muertos
comments.content:
Good evening JMG and All,

First of all, thank you to the ones that wrote to me about Día de los Muertos for showing interest and wanting to give it a try. And also thank you for letting me know most of the drama was in my head and not in my writing.

Dealing with the trauma that I lived in Seattle was still a few months ahead on the list but this season helped to face it and I would like to share some of my experience applying the OSA work to it.

I think I've mentioned before that I grew up, as many mexicanos do, looking up to the US, of our parents. My late grandfather Octavio Carbalahes studied his residency at a Children's Hospital off in Maryland.

While writing about it and the things I lived in poor Seattle, at some moment, the hand refused to write. It just stopped as in saying, ah, ah, careful. W. E Butler used to say that the relationship with our subconscious mind many times benefits from approaching it as with the card The Lovers, instead of someone on a quest to conquer something. That came to mind. So I started digging and pushing around the rocks somewhat clumsily until at some point: furious writing that is to this date ineligible happened, accompanied by a scratched primeval half growl half haul. That was interesting.

At that moment it just felt like, not all of it, but a big chunk of crud blasted off a pipe and let more water flow through. Admittedly it felt as if chunks of it stuck and drooled down the walls in repulse. And then it made me think about The Lovers, what if I had pushed it? Surgery requires some care, and trust, I guess.

All in all, it was very relieving, and revealing because almost all of a sudden my memories and thoughts and ideas I had of the country I loved where there within reach, even if some of them tentatively. A sense of energy was there too. Then I remembered part of the rich relationship our countries have had for centuries in trade, in the arts and in science, even in magic and the occult.

Did you know, for example, that Michael Bertiaux, born in Seattle to a prominent Theosophist, went to found the O.T.A --Ordo Templi Astartes that was part of the Gnostic Voodoo Church-- in Acapulco, Guerrero, a popular beach town in the Pacific? He probably was one of the first ones to explore the occult from a western perspective using ceremonial methods but also merging with non european sources of power. After the german doctor and occultist Arnold Krumm-Heller, who among other things sided with Francisco I. Madero, a Mexican president who also was a prominent spiritist and probably helped in the introduction of mexican Espiritismo which came from Europe and then mixed with shamanism, to the States.

Also, during the Cristero War in the late 1920's, which was anti-clerical, groups of American Wandering Bishops of Independent Catholic traditions went down to México and ordained priests and established lines of apostolic succession not linked with the Church leaving them outside the control of the Vatican. These lineages became intertwined with a mix of mexoamerican occult traditions that blended Catholicism with magic. And lastly, Timothy Leary, the famous psychedelic psychologist (The Psychedelic Psychologists ought to be a soft pop jazz band somewhere), first experienced with psilocybin mushrooms in my hometown of Cuernavaca, Morelos.

This was a lot of fun to retrace and value and allowed me to become lighter and also grateful of all the people here that have put on with my occasional snark, for their patience, kindness and from colluding with me from time to time.

Today on Día de Muertos I went to the capital's Zócalo, or main square, to see the decorations and festivities and also have a quick talk with a friend with which I had had a small grudge, before her performance. I couldn't watch the show but I went to one of the galleries of the museum next to Palacio Nacional and was struck by a mural towering over me showing many of the national heroes of all backgrounds, colors and races being empowered by technology Made in the U.S.A (zoom in) in its glory days and left me wandering (not a typo). What if we could make that happen again with partnerships of Mexican and American assembly lines? Or some other way that we can work together to create different blends talents and cultures and many Días de los Muertos? All this gives so much more meaning to all the suffering of those 8 years and its many serendipities and blessings. Wouldn't do it again, not the same way at least, might as well. Such are sometimes the demands of love, apache love, that is.

[IMAGE:0] [IMAGE:1]

This last one is a bird's eye view of a cemetery in Oaxaca. I hope everyone has had a blessed All Hallows Eve, Halloween, Día de Muertos, Samhain and Calan Tachwedd, to those who celebrate.

You can also find more pictures here. And some music

Listening to Llorona by Chavela Vargas and Besame Mucho by Lisa Ono with flowers, veladoras and booze, watching skeletons amongst the living. JMG, how was your Día de los Muertos party?

I'm the one who posted last week about the realization that someone I was in love with felt the same, and I blew it badly because I was convinced I was utterly unlovable.* I have a couple of followup questions that I did not get to because the realization this was what was happening was a little late in the day.

a) I am no longer sure who I am, because a lot of my self image has been based on a lie. I'm not sure what I want out of life anymore, because I no longer trust that I know myself well at all: I'm realizing that a lot of people actually find me a better person than I've allowed myself to see, and that I don't need to be lonely; but loneliness has been part of my self image since I was a child, and it's very disorienting to realize I don't actually have to be lonely: I've chosen to be lonely. While long term I think this will be great, it's pretty disruptive in the here and now.

It is also incredibly painful to realize that the reason someone I loved is no longer part of my life is because I pushed her away; to realize I almost certainly badly hurt her; that I still love her and miss her badly; and that as part of lashing out in response to this I destroyed a lot of important friendships. It hurts a great deal to realize just how many people I hurt, because I was being a fool, to use no stronger word (the best terms I could use about me would not be permissible here).

Do you have advice for dealing with these feelings constructively?

b) I've had past life memories from four different lives in various levels of clarity; all four of them checked the various tests I had for legitimacy (divination; explaining odd details of personality; including details I would never have known otherwise; and suggesting potential skills that I have developed in a few cases), but since this realization, all four of them have become much clearer, and all four of them appear to include a repeating pattern: there is always some opportunity for romance, which is then blown: one of us, or sometimes both of us, have a hard time admitting our feelings, and so we pushed each other away.

I am somehow quite confident that in each case, the person was also a previous life of the person I pushed away in this one. This seems like far too much of a coincidence, so is there a reason this would drag up past life memories that seem to indicate we've been doing this to each other for a long time?

Reading this thread Clarissa Pinkola Estes words came to mind:


Forget: Refuse to dwell; let go and loosen one's hold, particularly on memory. To forget is an active - not passive - endeavor.



To let go.
Some have a hard time
letting go of what is no longer,
what cannot be, what is not,
what has never been.

People say
‘just let go,
just let go,
just let go’
scattering the platitude like confetti
immediately swept away by any wind.

What is it exactly, this letting go?
No longer allowing the eye
to be caught by the hook…
No longer fastening the lock on the door,
just letting the door swing as it will…
No longer visiting the graves
where there is no love
and no blessing in both directions…
No longer reviewing and reviewing the past,
even the last moment,
as though there will be a test.
There will not be a test, dear soul.

What is it exactly,
this letting go?

Not reading the same chapter over and over
and over and over, futilely attempting
to make the indelible facts be rewritten…
Making new memories of quality
to bathe old scars and new life…
Moving into a larger world
in which the past
is but a dot on the landscape
rather than the only continent in sight.

We all find our ways…
letting go is shaking loose,
letting go is turning
in your great coat, into a new wind
forward into new sky and open road
leaving what cannot be,
and taking all treasure
from the wreck.

Each in her own way.
Each in his own way.

This comes with love. Hang in there. No one deserves to be nailing the hem of their cloak to the crossroad that once was, but that is not now.

Hi there,

As JMG said, my recent book, The Book of Sacramental Magic, provides an internal energetic practice that you might find suitable. It works specifically with the head, heart, and abdomen, which are the three energetic centers used in Taoist internal alchemy, but which are also well known (if these days somewhat obscure) in the Platonic tradition in both its Christian and pagan forms. (They're the centers of nous, thymos, and epithymia discussed in Plato's Republic, for example.)

If you're intent on working with the pentagram ritual and the Middle Pillar specifically, I also have some material that may help you. "JESU" works as a 4 letter name, but it's important to note that "JESU" is a genitive, so you aren't saying "Jesus," you're saying "Jesus's." I've developed a version of the pentagram ritual which uses Latin, rather than Hebrew, and uses "JESU" vibrated in each quarter, but in the form of a complete sentence. So, for example, in the Air quarter, you'd say, "In nomine [vibrate] JESU, regis caelorum, vadite, vadite phantasmata aerea! Purificetur oriens." You can find a detailed version of the ritual here: https://sacramentalmagic.substack.com/p/christian-ceremonial-magic-part-1

With a revised version, based on corrections by a reader whose Latin is better than mine, here: https://sacramentalmagic.substack.com/p/christian-ceremonial-magic-the-pentagram


I've also developed a version of the Middle Pillar itself which you might find helpful (I do). It can be read here: https://sacramentalmagic.substack.com/p/christian-ceremonial-magic-the-middle

The Names in this case are in Greek, and are taken from the Gospel of John. (Another ritual I have in the works uses Hebrew: Latin, Greek, and Hebrew are the 3 sacred languages of the Christian tradition, as these are the languages inscribed on the Cross.) They can be interpreted orthodoxically, or by reference to the doctrines of the Valentinian Gnostics, or both at once-- it works either way.

comments.title: Joe Dispenza
comments.content:
Hi JMG and all,

Joe Dispenza has come up a few times and I think he's been a bit hard done by. He very much comes across as part of the 'create your own reality' new age milieu with a side of quantum woo woo thrown in, however, there is a bit more roughage underlying what he is teaching. I've read two of his books, Breaking the Habit of Being Yourself and Becoming Supernatural, and found surprising depth and some useful information from an occult perspective.

The core of what he is teaching is working with elevated emotions and states of consciousness to seed the unconscious mind with desired experiences. His description of the ways that we become conditioned into rigid routines by barely noticed emotional responses to our environment and past experiences triggered some very useful reflection for me. He also explains how advertising emotionally manipulates our unconscious to get us to buy stuff. His material covers these processes in far more detail than you'll find in most occult texts and I found it worthwhile.

Another big focus is the importance of cultivating various meditative states of mind to facilitate manifesting desired circumstances. He has done a lot of work monitoring brain waves, heart activity and other bodily functions during meditative practices and has discovered that elevated states of consciousness and high vibing emotions like love and gratitude produce profound physiological changes, especially with repeated practice. The basic practices given to actively cultivate positive emotional states are probably worth doing for most people, given that he provides a lot of evidence to support the links between emotional and physical health.

His practices are limited to changing one's own consciousness in accordance with one's own will, and so pale in potential when compared with even beginner-mid level ceremonial magic practices, but as a guide to becoming aware of your own emotional programming and developing conscious control of it, along with some training in meditation and imagination, they are reasonable enough. It's bunny slope stuff, though, and for me the value was in the detailed discussions of the mechanics of thoughts, emotions and the unconscious.

He does, wildly, include a method for raising kundalini without any warnings in Becoming Supernatural - reader beware, as activation in some people can be disabling or deadly.

Thanks for Magic Mondays!

replies.title: Re: Joe Dispenza
replies.content:
I don't know if this matters for anything, but I think the obvious first guess about the heart rate variability thing that the HeartMath Institute promotes findings about is that it's the heart instinctively using low-stakes situations to try to get experimentally calibrated on its own responses and responses-to-responses to deviations from a perfect regular rhythm. That would help it become better oriented for what to do in the case of larger disturbances like potassium imbalance, blood loss, or heart attack. It'd be sort of like a micro-version of emergency drills. This is close to something that in control theory is called "system identification".

I'm not sure you could even usefully do that job of system identification and off-normal control policy updating with much less than 40,000 neurons, actually, unless the neurons were something closer to being each individually genetically coded, like the neurons in a flatworm. But anyway that's a strong hypothesis for what those neurons are mostly doing. Which would suggest that the memories are mostly about circumstances that affect/predict heart function or side instinctive inputs to heart function (at least sympathetic/parasympathetic nervous system activation, which is presumably a lot of where the heart's information about "low stakes" vs. "high stakes" is coming from).

But there is plausibly a distinction between a kind of transgenderism that shows up early and is more associated with separately-verifiable intersex conditions and a kind that shows up around puberty and is more associated with paraphilic conditions. The last time I skimmed two papers arguing opposite sides, the anti-Blanchardian side seemed to be throwing more smoke and more denying correlations and multimodal-mixture-probability-distribution structure. (Perhaps they thought that that was directionally correct activist argumentation, supposing-by-vibing that the alternative perspective logically led only to rigid single-point transgender categories and stigma against anyone deviant from those?)

My impression is that most of the new wave of transgender people have genuine dysphoria of some strong sort. Perhaps some of it is like the dysphoria that a cisgender person might feel from being desperately intensely unpretty/unrespected, and perhaps some of it is like the dysphoria of romantic loneliness but for oneself as the desired partner (in which cases "trans euphoria" is just new relationship energy?). Perhaps some of the uptick is because of estrogen-like effects of plastic, and perhaps some of the uptick is because of an external breakdown of a preference-falsification equilibrium leading to a breakdown of internalized preference-falsification structures -- you don't pine for what you never thought was a real option.

comments.title: Confusing combination of metaphysics
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

thanks for taking the time to answer our questions here. I really appreciate it.

Currently I am in a bit of a conundrum and could use some help untying a few knots:

I've started the work of the Golden Section Fellowship. At the same time I am in the slow process of becoming a Freemason (That would be European continental, regular Freemasonry, hence the interest in the material of the Fellowship of the Golden Section, since continental Freemasonry is fairly on the rationalist side of things - but that's the one available where I live.) I figured these two practices should go well together and I see no reason, why they shouldn't.

However, since there is some mention of incorporating other deities/deity into the practice (and some related Druid concepts) in the FGS-material I thought about doing that. I have had an interest in the norse pantheon for a while but couldn't really relate to the cosmology. (Similarly I can't relate to trinitarian Christianity.) So, I figured, I'd read your book about polytheism (with which I am almost through now) in order to better understand how to square different metaphysical/ontological concepts from different traditions. But so far, I have not succeeded.

My questions would thus be:

1) How do you do that? You work (or at least have worked) with very different traditions at the same time. How do you cognitively manage the differences between them? Do you have any connecting elements that you use to bridge the gaps/differences?

2) In practical terms: How for example would the "Great Architect of the Universe" fit into a cosmology like that of European Paganism? (I am having a real hard time imagining a coherent mental model forming from these different traditions.)

3) Am I going about this the wrong way? Would there need to be a coherent model that one aligns oneself to? Or would one switch perspectives depending on the practices one does?

For now I figured that trying to resolve this cognitively by myself is futile and I would likely just practice the FGS material in the basic "non-religious" versions you provide and let the rest figure itself out in due time. But I would nonetheless be grateful for your thoughts and perspective on the matter.

Thank you very much!

- Florian

replies.title: Re: Confusing combination of metaphysics
replies.content:
1) My underlying "metaphysics" is actually a rejection of metaphysics. I do not believe that human beings have the capacity to understand metaphysical realities. All we have are a series of fragmentary models that more or less fit our experience. That different models apply to different sets of experiences makes perfect sense in this context.

2) Good heavens, that's not even difficult. Here's the third and fourth stanzas from the Voluspa:

Of old was the age  when Ymir lived;
Sea nor cool waves  nor sand there were;
Earth had not been,  nor heaven above,
But a yawning gap,  and grass nowhere.

Then Bur's sons lifted  the level land,
Midgard the mighty  there they made;
The sun from the south  warmed the stones of earth,
And green was the ground  with growing leeks.

That is, the Aesir (Bur's sons") made the world. Who among the Aesir had the intellect to serve as architect? Why, Odin All-Father, of course. Within a European Pagan context, he is the Great Architect of the Universe, full stop, end of sentence.

3) I've just given you two options -- the via negativa and via analogia, if you'd like their technical names. Nor are they mutually exclusive!

replies.title: Re: Confusing combination of metaphysics
replies.content:
Re 2, and I realise that you'll probably point to your rejection of metaphysics in response, now I'm having trouble combining hard polytheism and the understanding that most religions are in some sense true. If we view the Voluspa as more than metaphorical, we'd have to either privilege it above other creation myths (and I can't imagine that that's what you're getting at) or we need to accept that other creation myths are equally valid.

But assuming that Odin and Yahweh are distinct, that implies they've both created the universe, which strikes me as paradoxical. So how do either of them get to be the Great Architect?

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Confusing combination of metaphysics
replies.content:
That depends on your definition of hard polytheism. If your version of hard polytheism requires one polytheist faith to be literally true to the exclusion of all others, you're going to land yourself in a mess long before you get to Voluspa. Is the god of thunder Thor, Perun, Shango, Indra, Lei Gong, or someone else? On the other hand, if you accept that Thor, Perun, et al. are all distinct divine personalities with whom people can interact, that all of them seem to have something to do with thunder, and that as human beings we have no more ability to figure out how that works than the dust mites in Mozart's wig had of figuring out his music, it all works just fine.

What we know about the creation of the universe isn't much. We have myths that assign this task to various deities, and that's as far as we can go. For what it's worth, I don't think that anybody's myths are literally true, but then I don't think that geology textbooks are literally true, either...

replies.title: Re: Confusing combination of metaphysics
replies.content:
Right, that's what I meant by combining hard polytheism and the understanding that most religions are in some sense true: Clearly, both Thor and Perun exist (though I'd be willing to accept that those two are actually the same being, so perhaps Thor and Krishna would be a better example). That leaves one in a bit of an awkward spot trying to find a creation myth that's literally true as they're all contradictory (and, for what it's worth, the geology textbook accounts for the lack of a giant tree much better than the Voluspa, so I'd wager it's closer to at least the material truth).

Either way, literal truth isn't all that essential to my confusion, which might simply be a misconception. The Great Architect has been described to me (by a Freemason whom I told of my desire to become one too) as a supreme being. Clearly, Odin and Yahweh, to return to my example, can't both be supreme (which is fine, because, like the anon who mentioned The Cosmic Doctrine said, I can fall back to the Galactic Logos). But since you don't appear to see this problem, perhaps this description was just a bit too coloured by a Christian perspective?

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Confusing combination of metaphysics

replies.title: Re: Confusing combination of metaphysics
replies.content:
Questions about contradictions like that seem, for better or worse, less pressing when one is not under the goad of the idea that one might be somehow doing monstrous and horrifically-punishable evil by giving service and worship to a being as supreme that wasn't supreme.

I don't know if this will help, but you might try approaching the problem of "what even is a distinction between beings?" from a meta-level. If you imagine a population of people each independently exposed to fuzzy pictures of pairs of mountain peaks that were at varying distances and relative heights from each other, some of the people will come away calling it "one mountain" (perhaps with a sub-peak) and some will come away calling it "two mountains", and perhaps a few will come away saying something like "I wasn't sure whether to say it was one mountain or two" (or even "it's weird how I wasn't sure whether to say it was one mountain or two"). And the probability distribution of how many people will respond one way or another will depend on the relative distances and heights of the mountain peaks, the angle of the picture, and the degree of fuzziness. If discourse about that rock formation somehow then turned out to have moral valence, then the culpability or merit of people speaking of them one way or another would then have to be relative to what kind of information they had had access to. (Of course then that just leads into situations of culturally-contested moral valence, and people saying "it's weird how the ambiguity of whether to say it's one mountain or two lines up so cleanly with my internal motivational factions and conflicting cultural-memeplex sympathies accusing each other of bad faith".)

In fact, the relative probabilities of people classifying the rock formation one way or another, and indeed the benefits or drawbacks of thinking of the rock formation one way or another, would have to exhibit continuous variation with respect to continuous variation of the positions of the peaks and the properties of the pictures. Likewise the relative degrees of culpability or merit, in the case where discourse about the rock formation had moral valence. Investigating the structure of these continuous variations in thought experiments might help a bit with understanding what the rules were for juggling more complicated conflicting cosmologies.

And then, of course, mountains are "passive" and aren't really engineering plans around your states of representation and nomenclature, whereas divinity-personalities are instead doing some weird other thing. Why have some people have been left with ambiguous information lending itself to the interpretation that that hegemonic cosmology is morally obligatory while other people have been left with ambiguous information lending itself to the interpretation that pluralistic cosmology is both morally permissible and (relatively more) truthful? I can only suppose that the divine doesn't see fit to exclude cultural conflict between cosmology-based memeplexes from the human experience, even though at first face that seems like a betrayal of trust.

replies.title: Re: Confusing combination of metaphysics
replies.content:
There's a trap here that almost everyone falls into. We generally suppose that any two things are either one and the same thing or two different things. Also, we suppose that a thing either exists or doesn't exist. That works well enough for the world in which we usually live, the realm of time and space, of matter and energy.

But there is another realm, too: a realm in which logic does not lead to truth, but regularly leads to error. This realm, too, is a realm which simply cannot be perceived by any recognized human sense organ, nor described with even minimal adequacy in any human language. Most people go through their entire lives completely oblivious to this other realm. A few people experience occasions when they perceive this world not through any senses any of the body, but by what I call "direct perception," that is, perception not mediated by one's body or any part of it. Sometimes this comes to a person as a simple, temporary gift. Sometimes a person attains it by long hard apophatic mystical practice.

This other realm is, I suppose, the realm in which the Gods Themselves have their existence. They only visit our realm for shorter or longer periods (often out of kindness), and also they watch our realm from their own realm.

This is why one can truly say that, for example, Odin and Yahweh both are and are not one and the same Grand Architect of the Universe. This is also why any detailed theology expressed in any human language is flawed and inadequate: always has been, and always will be, world without end.

As for direct perception itself, I can speak from hours-long experience of it, twice a few days apart when I was 13 and an a know-it-all loud-mouthed kid. It was definitely a free gift, given for the Gods' own wholly opaque purposes; I had done nothing whatever to merit it. Those long-ago hours were far and away the most real experience I have ever had in all my 83 years, and the experience that has had the strongest impact on the course of my life.

And there is no way I can describe or explain what I perceived during those hours in any language I have ever studied.

replies.content:
This is Winifred, responding to Florian’s statement “I have had an interest in the norse pantheon for a while but couldn't really relate to the cosmology,” and the question “In practical terms: How for example would the "Great Architect of the Universe" fit into a cosmology like that of European Paganism?”

Florian, I have a complete fascination with Norse cosmology and have spent the past thirty-plus years as a Heathen philosopher and theologian, deep-diving into it, seeing fractal unfoldings of multiple meanings the deeper I go. I’ve just published my fifth book about various aspects of Heathen cosmology and metaphysics, among other topics, this one focused on the Norns, orlog, and Wyrd, and how they shape the World. My first two books are on the subject of souls and how they come into being, which is connected with cosmology and cosmogony, of course.

I can understand your confusion in trying to work with different levels and sources of metaphor, explanation, and understanding of these matters. I am a retired scientist, a research ecologist, but am also a very spiritual person. It took me years of really painful struggle with multiple cognitive dissonances before I arrived at my present state of full comfort with seemingly contradictory world-views. It can be done, though, and is worth the effort! For me, it only happened once I went through the gradual process of converting to the Heathen faith and worldview and began exploring deeper, metaphorical meanings behind the myths along with my spiritual practice.

My ‘secret,’ if you will, is to fully accept the following philosophical understanding: Reality is comprised of multiple / infinite layers or fractal morphisms, far more than any humans can fully identify, explore, and comprehend—individually or collectively. We choose one or several paths through this cognitive jungle: this is what specific religions, philosophies, and esoteric practices do, and all of those paths are valid ways of comprehending some aspects of Reality. It’s a mistake to fight over different interpretations because all of them are true; it depends on the paths we choose to dedicate ourselves to. All of them are worthy of respect.

Many people prefer to walk a single path without major contradictions or disconnects, though some will always be present. For example, religions that have doctrines and dogma, or philosophies based on clear-cut syllogisms. Others, like me and many others, are drawn to explore several seemingly contradictory paths together, because of a deep fascination about where they might lead us.

I explore Heathen / Norse cosmology both through religious metaphors -- like Odin, Vili, and Ve creating Midgard from Ymir’s sacrifice, the Norns laying the layers of orlog from which all phenomena arise, or Mimir’s head / Well as the Noösphere -- but also through grand cosmological imaginings of the workings of ‘alternative’ physics, quantum theory, alchemy, cosmic ecology, and the like. Here is one example, from the beginning of my article “The Awakening of the Souls.”

“There is Fire and there is Ice. They form the poles of Ginnungagap, the Gap of Magical Potential, the great void that holds all potential Being within itself. The polarities of heat and cold meet in the middle and form a vast space of roiling mist and steam: this is Mist-World or Niflheim. Here, all-that-might-be exists as wisps and shifting forms and energies, ever flowing and reshaping. Here is where Ahma arises, also called Ond and Athom: the sacred breath of Spirit that flows through all existence.

Through this space of Ginnungagap currents of enormous power flow, generated by the primal polarities, and in the center they meet and swirl into a gigantic, churning fountain of energy called Hvergelmir, the Roaring Cauldron. Up and out it bursts, perpetually re-creating space-time by its existence and motion. This is the source of all the forces operating in the Worlds, flowing forth in the shape of the Elivagar, the mighty rivers of energy that feed the Worlds and form the turbulent boundaries between them.

As Hvergelmir’s energy-fountain billows up, it begins its first stage of condensation, forming the World of Hel, the Womb of Souls. Hel is not the dumping ground of the dead. Rather, it is here that the potentialities of Mist-World, all the swirling Might-Be’s, take the first steps toward becoming beings in their own right.”

There is more along these lines in my six-part series on “The Alchemy of Hel,” in other articles I’m getting ready to post now like “The Quantum Nature of the Norns’ Work,” and “The Work of the Three Wells” where I talk about entropic inversion, as well as others of my articles. The latter two articles and a great deal more are from my new book, "Orlog Yesterday and Today: The Shapings of the Norns," which is in the process of also being posted on my website as individual articles. It covers subjects such as causality, determinism, predestination, fate, Time and Space, cosmology, etc.

Perhaps all these topics might relate to your question about envisioning “The Great Architect of the Universe” in different terms that would mesh better with your interest in Norse lore? My website is HeathenSoulLore.net, and if you perchance have any interest in corresponding about this you can find the page for Questions and Comments there.

Best wishes on your path!
Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: Trauma Release Exercise
comments.content:
I stumbled across this exercise called "Trauma Release Exercise" last week. Someone on Twitter reported that their friend who was always "skittish", lost all her skittishness after she started doing this.

You don't do YouTube so I'll briefly describe it here.

Basically, you slightly fatigue out your legs by lying down, putting the soles of your feet together and raise your hips. If you are fitter you can do something close to a full bridge. Do it for about one minute. Then rest with your hips on the ground for another minute. Then slowly bring your hips towards each other, they will start "tremoring" on their own.

It's a very similar experience to when you sit on a chair, with your feet on the ground, then slowly tiptoe up. At some point you will naturally shake.

The exercise is about letting the tremors spread throughout your body from the legs up to your head.

That's the basic technique which they teach for free online. They offer consultations but the creator of the technique himself says it's for people with specific trauma or other requirements that would be helpful to have a coach.

The basic theory as far as I understand it, is that traumas, emotional shocks, and other negative experiences are tied with your body, and especially the limbic system. Animals in the wild naturally tremble and tremor after a shocking experience, e.g. an antelope who escapes a lion, when it's in a safe place, will tremor for a while and get on with life. Humans and domesticated animals have lost this instinct and PTSD among others is one manifestation of not letting the limbic system shake through and release this.

I've only done this a few times since last week but found it very beneficial so far. It relieved most of this low level brain fog that I've had for quite a while, I managed to have the tremors spread up to my hands and neck.

My question is, based on your occult knowledge, are there any risks of this exercise? I am completely conscious throughout the process and can stop at any time.

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditation

Would you mind elaborating a bit on discursive meditation ?

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditation
replies.content:
I've done a series of posts on discursive meditation here:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/tag/discursive+meditation

What sets it apart from most other kinds of meditation is that you choose a theme -- a concept, image, or text -- for the focus, and then engage with it mentally; you don't just let thoughts go drifting past, you direct and explore them, while keeping them focused on the theme. I promise you this is just as difficult as dealing with wandering thoughts in other forms of meditation! The result is that over time, with practice, your thinking mind becomes an ally and not an enemy of your spiritual work. It's also worth noting that many of the texts and symbols of Western esoteric spirituality are meant to be unpacked using this method, and don't yield up their inner teachings until this is done.

Hi JMG. I’ve been consistent in my regular Golden Dawn foundational practice, and I genuinely enjoy it. However, I’m wondering whether I should continue with it as is or consider adding to it.

First, in your experience, is practical and theurgic work done alongside a personal Golden Dawn practice truly effective or potent? I ask because I know you’ve moved from the traditional Golden Dawn system to the work of the Golden Fellowship, and the only clear overlap I see between the two is the practice of scrying, pathworking, and divination. I’m quite happy with my horary divination practice, though I completely understand your comment about it sometimes being overwhelmingly complex—it can definitely feel head-spinning at times.

This brings me to a broader question about the inherent power of the Golden Dawn system itself. I’m not sure if this is due to gaps in my own understanding or execution, but a practitioner I deeply respect—who works extensively with texts like the Picatrix and the Greek Magical Papyri—once told me he regretted spending years performing the order’s pentagram and hexagram rituals decades ago. From what I gathered, he did not include the Middle Pillar exercise in his daily routine, which might have provided the spiritual sustenance or energetic foundation those rituals otherwise lack on their own. That’s made me reflect on whether the core GD rituals, when practiced in isolation, are truly sufficient for building real magical capacity over time.

First, I simply do not have the time or resources to craft the elaborate ritual tools described in Regardie’s books or their equivalents in your Circles of Power—things like wands, chalices, and pentacles. More importantly, I’m unsure whether these physical tools actually contribute meaningfully to the effectiveness or power of the work. Could you speak to their practical necessity? Have to admit, their cartoony tools seem... unrealistic.

Second, I’ve struggled to defend the Golden Dawn’s potency to this person , mainly because I haven’t yet experienced clear, tangible results from my own practice that I can point to as evidence. That said, I’ve read Lyam Thomas Christopher’s red book *Kabbalah Magic*, which is rooted in the Golden Dawn framework, and I've heard accounts of practitioners achieving profound, life-changing transformations following it. While I respect that work, I still prefer your *Circles of Power* system for its clarity and coherence.

Third—and this was shared with me by another highly effective magician—the original founders of the Golden Dawn apparently cobbled together fragments from earlier traditions such as Rosicrucianism and Martinism, mixed in Theosophical interpretations, and layered on a flawed understanding of Egyptian mythology. The result, according to this view, was a syncretic and somewhat inconsistent curriculum. I’m genuinely uncertain how to weigh this criticism against the system’s enduring influence and the real experiences of those who swear by it. Not to mention Crowley's horrible reputation which sadly also affects the Order.

Fourth, there’s the critique from traditional Kabbalists. Many of them argue that the Golden Dawn’s version of Kabbalah is fundamentally broken, inaccurate, and bears little resemblance to authentic Jewish Kabbalah. While it’s true that the system incorporates names of angels familiar in Talmudic and Rabbinic tradition—such as Michael, Raphael, Gabriel, and Uriel—the underlying framework is largely drawn from Martinism and Rosicrucianism, not from the lived, textual, or initiatic lineage of Jewish mysticism. Those sources were developed by esoteric Christians with limited access to, or understanding of, the original Kabbalistic corpus, which raises serious questions about the theological and metaphysical coherence of the GD’s adaptation. One look at their 'Neo Enochian' (Golden Dawn Enochian) material is enough to validate this.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
0) I'm not surprised that the person you mention had negative experiences, if he neglected the Middle Pillar exercise. I also wonder if he did much with the meditations in the system. Too many modern GD practitioners neglect daily discursive meditation; the founders of the GD don't seem to have realized that not everyone learns about that from their Anglican vicar (discursive meditation was a normal part of religious education in Anglican churches in Victorian England) and so contented themselves with hints. All the knowledge lectures are meant to be used as a basis for meditation.

1) The working tools are worth making and consecrating. They have a talismanic effect, becoming reservoirs of energy and consciousness that strengthen the student's work. I find it very hard to believe, btw, that you really can't find the time and resources to make at least the very simple versions of the tools set out in Circles of Power. I've heard this before, of course, but in every case I had the chance to investigate personally, the operative word was not "can't" but "won't."

2) All I can say here is that the GD system was in fact life-changing for me, and in a very positive way. I got excellent results from its magical workings; more importantly, the daily routine of practices helped me enormously over time. I wound up my GD work partly because I'd done the whole system, partly because I wanted to devote time to the material I was studying with my teacher John Gilbert, and partly because I realized (as a result of my work with John) that it was possible to do the same things in other ways, and I wanted to explore those.

3) The proof of the pudding is in the eating. The proof of the magical system is in what it can accomplish. In a way, Crowley's career is a kind of sidelong testament to the power of the system. Many years ago I read a wry article pointing out that his horoscope was that of a bland, meek, uninteresting little man. He certainly overcame that destiny through magic!

4) One of the least pleasant features of all the Abrahamic religions is their habit of badmouthing their rivals, and this is an example of that. Rosicrucianism and Martinism have their own lived, textual, and initiatic lineages going back a good many centuries; for that matter, it's a matter of public record (discussed by Gershom Scholem among others) that the Cabala wasn't originally Jewish, and was borrowed from the Gnostics in the same way that Christian Cabalists later borrowed it from the Jews. (The Gnostics, of course, borrowed it themselves from Middle Platonist and Neopythagorean mystics in ancient times.) What makes a spiritual tradition valid is not its access to this or that literary corpus but its links to the transcendent Spirit, and these the Hermetic and Rosicrucian lineages that flowed into the GD definitely had.

More broadly speaking, it's possible to denounce any esoteric tradition using the sort of arguments you've rehearsed here. Turn the same standards on the Picatrix, or the PGM, or the traditional Jewish Cabala, or any other esoteric tradition anywhere, and you can make exactly the same case -- they're all syncretic, patched together out of raw materials from various sources, questionably coherent, and shaped by individual practitioners who (like all human beings everywhere) have their own biases and limitations. That's the nature of the material that comes down to us. If you're trying to build a house, you don't worry about whether the tools you can gather are all from the same "authentic" manufacturer -- what matters is whether they do the job. I submit that the same logic should rule here as well.

Escape rooms have an interesting history: they're real-world emulations of a computer game genre, which is loosely based on a genre of TV game shows from the 80's and 90's that are themselves loosely based on the heist and treasure-hunter genres, where the protagonist(s) have to solve a set of intricate and/or cryptic puzzles to secure a valuable treasure and then get out in the nick of time.
